data.title: Foxconn Technology Group August Revenue NT$606.51B, Up 11% on Year
Asian currencies, stocks climb on Fed cut hopes, weak dollar
data.description:
Key points:
  • Asian FX rallies on Fed rate-cut bets, weak dollar
  • Taiwan dollar, Korean won lead Asian FX higher
  • Regional stocks climb, led by Manila, Taipei

Asian currencies strengthened against the U.S. dollar on Friday as traders positioned for a Federal Reserve rate cut this month, while equities posted broad gains following Wall Street's overnight rally to record highs.

The South Korean won strengthened 0.35% and Taiwan's dollar climbed 0.49%, leading regional currencies. Taiwan's benchmark stock index TWSE:TAIEX rose 1.3%, extending its winning streak as technology stocks rallied.

In Thailand, the parliament was set to choose a new prime minister after a court's dismissal of Paetongtarn Shinawatra from the post. The baht gained 0.25% against the greenback, while stocks in Bangkok advanced 0.99% as markets looked beyond the political uncertainty.

Trade, however, remained subdued with Malaysia and Indonesia observing public holidays.

Markets have all but priced in a quarter-point Fed rate cut at next week's policy meeting, with traders expecting 60 basis points of reductions by year-end.

The dollar index fell 0.2% to 98.11 as traders awaited U.S. jobs data later in the day. The Fed meets on September 16 and 17.

"We anticipate the Federal Reserve will ease policy over the coming months, providing room for Asian central banks to cut rates to support growth amid trade headwinds," MUFG's forex strategist Lloyd Chan said.

"Against a backdrop of a weaker U.S. dollar, we favour the yen, rupiah, and ringgit."

On Thursday, the Bank Negara Malaysia left its overnight policy rate unchanged at 2.75% as expected.

Expectations of an easier monetary environment have supported global equities. The S&P 500 rose 0.8% to finish at a record high on Thursday.

Most regional stock markets rose, with the Philippines' index climbing 0.69% and Singapore's Straits Times adding 0.38%. Japan's Nikkei gained 0.19%, while India's Nifty declined 0.43%.

The MSCI gauge of emerging market currencies (.MIEM00000CUS) was little changed this week, while a gauge of emerging Asian equities (.MIMS00000PUS) advanced more than 1%, on track for its best week since August 11.

Though Indonesian markets had some respite this week, they could face headwinds again as deadly protests over lawmakers' bonuses escalated after police killed a motorcycle taxi driver during demonstrations.

Rights groups have reported 10 deaths and over 1,000 injuries in clashes with security forces.

The rupiah gained 0.4% over four sessions on broad dollar weakness after losing more than 1% due to the protests last week. Jakarta stocks rose 0.5% this week, after losing 0.4% in the previous week.

HIGHLIGHTS:

** Indonesia to discuss fuel supply issue with private distributors

** China's central bank to inject 1 trillion yuan

data.title: REG - TempletonEmerg.Mkt. - Net Asset Value(s)
data.description:
RNS Number : 2101Y Templeton Emerging Markets IT PLC 05 September 2025  Templeton Emerging Markets Investment Trust PLC ('TEMIT')         On behalf of TEMIT, Franklin Templeton Investment Trust Management Limited reports the unaudited cum-income net asset value ('NAV') of TEMIT as at     04 September 2025 was  £2162.400m, representing a NAV of 221.46 pence per share. The unaudited ex-income NAV of TEMIT as at 04 September 2025 was  £2132.796m, representing a NAV of 218.42 pence per share.     In accordance with the Association of Investment Companies recommendations, the cum-income NAV has been calculated based on the total value of underlying assets,    including accumulated or accrued income, and using bid price information relating to the underlying assets.    The ex-income NAV has been calculated based on the total value of underlying assets, excluding these income elements, and using bid price information relating to the underlying assets.         Portfolio data for TEMIT can be found on the website www.TEMIT.co.uk.         For information please contact Client Dealer Services on freephone 0800 305 306.    This information is provided by RNS, the news service of the London Stock Exchange. RNS is approved by the Financial Conduct Authority to act as a Primary Information Provider in the United Kingdom. data.title: Spain's Markets Regulator: Prospectus Content Is Sufficient After Latest Amendments
Spain's Markets Regulator: Bid Terms Comply With Regulations
LVMH's Results Should Mirror Challenging Luxury Trends — Market Talk
data.description: LVMH's fortunes in the months ahead should remain strongly correlated with industry trends, Jefferies analysts write in a note. It remains tough to construct a compelling case for luxury, or the French conglomerate, they say. The sector continues to face a slowdown in demand for high-end goods, particularly in China, which drove the industry's growth for years. Despite this, Jefferies expects LVMH's key fashion and leather goods division to report improved trends for the third quarter, with a less pronounced decline in sales than in the prior quarter. "The overall uncertainty about consumers' willingness to spend at the higher price points in the U.S. and Europe and the extent to which the less affluent Chinese consumer will rebound, will be critical," they say. Shares fall 1.2%. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

data.title: Lululemon slides as profit outlook tightens again
data.description:
** Shares of Lululemon Athletica fall 17.37% to $170.3 in premarket trading

** Co slashed its annual profit forecast for the second quarter in a row on Thursday

** Expects 2025 net revenue to be in the range of $10.85 bln to $11 bln, compared with its prior forecast of $11.15 bln to $11.30 bln

** Analysts at Piper Sandler say that Lululemon's U.S. and Canada sales are trending slightly negative, pressured by weak product execution and rising competition from Alo Yoga

** While LULU is raising prices to offset margin hits, analysts doubt its pricing power amid heavier promotions and off-mark assortments

** Shares of peer Nike down 1.3% on low vols

** Stock rated "buy" on average by 33 analysts; median PT $251 - data compiled by LSEG

** Up to last close, stock is down ~46% YTD

data.title: Sanofi's Eczema Drug Candidate Could Still Become a Blockbuster — Market Talk
data.description: Sanofi posted solid results of a new eczema drug trial that support the medicine's potential to hit annual peak sales of 5 billion euros, Berenberg analysts write. The trial results triggered a negative share reaction, but Sanofi's amlitelimab could still capture a 4% share of the eczema market in patients that already received an initial treatment, the analysts says. Amlitelimab's dosing schedule of four injections a year is attractive in a nascent treatment market with tremendous growth potential, making this a compelling opportunity for the French pharmaceutical company to deliver the safest treatment with superior dosing convenience, they add. "We consider this a good outcome in a market where stellar Dupixent data were unlikely to be repeated," Berenberg says, referring to the drug jointly developed by Sanofi and Regeneron. Sanofi shares rise 0.2%. (william.gray@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0823 GMT - Sanofi posted solid results of a new eczema drug trial that support the medicine's potential to hit annual peak sales of 5 billion euros, Berenberg analysts write. The trial results triggered a negative share reaction, but Sanofi's amlitelimab could still capture a 4% share of the eczema market in patients that already received an initial treatment, the analysts says. Amlitelimab's dosing schedule of four injections a year is attractive in a nascent treatment market with tremendous growth potential, making this a compelling opportunity for the French pharmaceutical company to deliver the safest treatment with superior dosing convenience, they add. "We consider this a good outcome in a market where stellar Dupixent data were unlikely to be repeated," Berenberg says, referring to the drug jointly developed by Sanofi and Regeneron. Sanofi shares rise 0.2%. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0822 GMT - LVMH's fortunes in the months ahead should remain strongly correlated with industry trends, Jefferies analysts write in a note. It remains tough to construct a compelling case for luxury, or the French conglomerate, they say. The sector continues to face a slowdown in demand for high-end goods, particularly in China, which drove the industry's growth for years. Despite this, Jefferies expects LVMH's key fashion and leather goods division to report improved trends for the third quarter, with a less pronounced decline in sales than in the prior quarter. "The overall uncertainty about consumers' willingness to spend at the higher price points in the U.S. and Europe and the extent to which the less affluent Chinese consumer will rebound, will be critical," they say. Shares fall 1.2%. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

0820 GMT - Broadcom shares surge Friday premarket after the maker of semiconductor and software products posted better-than-expected results for its fiscal third quarter. Broadcom stock closed 1.2% higher at $306.10 ahead of results on Thursday. Shares are up 7.3% at $328.48 Friday premarket. The company reported $15.95 billion in revenue for the quarter ended Aug. 3, up 22% on year. Analysts had forecast $15.82 billion in revenue, according to FactSet. Adjusted Ebitda came in at $10.70 billion, above a FactSet forecast of $10.47 billion. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0819 GMT - Mainland China's stock market isn't likely to see a repeat of the boom and bust experienced in 2015, Julius Baer analyst Richard Tang writes in a note. Chinese regulators' views toward the stock market have become more constructive, Tang says. Over the past year, authorities have included supporting asset prices as part of their key policies, he adds. This is different from 10 years ago, when policies focused on minimizing downside risks in the stock market, Tang says. The stock market isn't as frothy as it was in 2015, Tang adds. The rally remains concentrated in a handful of growth sectors such as technology, and hasn't broadened to other sectors, Tang says. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0816 GMT - It could be difficult for Berkeley Group to achieve the targets outlined in its 2035 strategy, Interactive Investor analyst Richard Hunter says in a research note. The U.K. housebuilder has much working in its favor but ultimately operates in a cyclical sector, which is currently constrained by the wider economic environment, he says. For potential buyers, the level of interest rates and general affordability are under some strain, he adds. "For the time being investors remain unconvinced by the recovery potential, with the market consensus stuck at a hold, albeit a strong one," Hunter says. Shares trade 1.7% higher at 3640 pence. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

0813 GMT - Orsted's downgrade to earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization is a small negative ahead of Friday's vote on a proposed 60 billion Danish kroner rights issue, Jefferies analyst Ahmed Farman writes. The company lowered its full-year Ebitda guidance to between 24 billion and 27 billion kroner, from 25 billion-28 billion kroner previously, driven by lower-than-normal offshore wind speeds during July and August. Orsted also mentioned it expects a construction delay at its Greater Changhua 2b project, which will also adversely affect Ebitda in 2025. Jefferies expects details on the rights issue in the coming days. Shares rise 0.9%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0808 GMT - U.S. non-farm payrolls data due to be released at 1230 GMT are expected to show that jobs growth cooled in August but wasn't severely weak, RBC Capital Markets strategists say in a note. RBC expects the data to show a "cool but not frozen 64,000 jobs added in August," they say. However, any significant revisions to jobs figures for previous months could add to market volatility, RBC says. The consensus forecast in a WSJ poll predicts that the U.S. economy created 75,000 jobs in August, after 73,000 jobs were created in July. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0759 GMT - Chinese shares ended higher on Friday amid positive investor sentiment. The index rebounded after Thursday's selloff following a media report that Chinese regulators were mulling options to cool down the stock market. Solar stocks led the gains with Sungrow Power Supply and EVE Energy up 17% each. CSI Solar gained 13%. Lithium stocks were also higher with Ganfeng Lithium up 10% and Tinaqi Lithium adding 8.7%. Meanwhile, Kunlun Tech led the declines, falling 5.6%. Bank stocks were also lower with Postal Savings Bank of China down 3.0% and China CITIC Bank Corp declining 2.6%. The benchmark Shanghai Composite Index ended 1.2% higher at 3812.51, the Shenzhen Composite Index added 3.2% and the ChiNext Price Index gained 6.6%. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0742 GMT - Gold prices edge higher in early trade after pausing from a record-breaking rally as traders locked in profits ahead of the release of key U.S. nonfarm payrolls data. Futures are up 0.1% to $3,610.40 a troy ounce and on track for a 2.6% weekly gain, after hitting a new high of $3,640.10/oz earlier this week. The precious metal's surge was largely driven by expectations that the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates at its next meeting, concerns about the U.S. central bank's independence following President Trump's move to fire Fed governor Lisa Cook, and persistent geopolitical risks. Now, "the biggest risk from a market perspective is an upside surprise to today's payrolls number," says Kathleen Brooks, research director at XTB. "If rate cut bets get scaled back, then inflation fears could ease, which may hurt the world's most famous inflation hedge." (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0727 GMT - Temenos's earlier-than-expected CEO transition isn't entirely surprising, Vontobel says in a research note. The Swiss banking software company said Jean-Pierre Brulard, who took the helm in May last year, is stepping down and will be replaced on an interim basis by CFO Takis Spiliopoulos. No substantial further changes at the management team are expected after Brulard's departure despite the numerous appointments under his leadership, analyst Michael Foeth writes. "We had not considered the appointment of Jean-Pierre Brulard as a long-term solution in the first place," he says, adding that Spiliopoulos stepping in ensures continuity and that the CFO could be a potential candidate himself. Temenos needs a longer-term perspective and should announce a definitive solution sooner rather than later, he says. Shares open 13% lower. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0715 GMT - Ashmore shares open 12% lower at 150 pence, wiping the London-listed emerging markets asset manager's year-to-date gains after it reported its full-year print. Adjusted Ebitda was lower than expectations on heavier staff costs while its top line was weaker as management fees also missed views, Jefferies says in a research note. Performance fees were in line. Emerging markets demand should be helped by a weaker dollar and concerns about how much debt developed countries have, analysts say. Signs of this have emerged over the past four to five months, and this should be positive for Ashmore, they add. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0708 GMT - Orsted's guidance downgrade is a negative ahead of the rights issue prospectus which is expected early next week, RBC analyst Alexander Wheeler writes. Following lower-than-normal offshore wind speeds, Orsted lowered its 2025 Ebitda guidance to 24 billion-27 billion Danish kroner ($3.7 billon to $4.2 billion), from 25 billion-28 billion kroner previously. Company consensus is at 26.5 billion kroner, Wheeler adds. Lower wind speeds have impacted Ebitda by 1.2 billion kroner, with a construction delay at the Greater Changhua 2b project providing a 300 million kroner hit. Additionally, commissioning of Greater Changhua 2b has been delayed from late 2025 to the third quarter of 2026 due to damage to an export cable, he adds. Shares fall 1.7%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: Taiwan Inflation Climbs to 4-Month High
data.description:
The annual inflation rate in Taiwan rose slightly to 1.60% in August from 1.54% in July, the highest reading in four months, yet still below the central bank’s 2% target.

The uptick was primarily driven by faster price increases in food (3.18% vs 2.91% in July), as recent typhoons and heavy rainfall caused agricultural losses, pushing vegetable, meat, and food-away-from-home prices higher.

Prices also rose further in housing (1.8% vs 1.75%), miscellaneous goods and services (2.59% vs 2.06%), and health (1.75% vs 1.66%).

In contrast, the overall increase was partially offset by moderated price growth in education and entertainment (1.26% vs 1.45%) and a further slowdown in transportation and communication prices (-1.34% vs -0.89%).

On a seasonally adjusted monthly basis, consumer prices rose 0.12% in August, easing from a 0.24% increase in July.

Over the past year, authorities have included supporting asset prices as part of their key policies, he adds. Public disclosures under Rule 8 of the Code must be made to a Regulatory Information Service.

The Panel's Market Surveillance Unit is available for consultation in relation to the Code's disclosure requirements on +44 (0)20 7638 0129.

*If the discloser is a natural person, a telephone number does not need to be included, provided contact information has been provided to the Panel's Market Surveillance Unit.

The Code can be viewed on the Panel's website at www.thetakeoverpanel.org.uk.

Citigroup Global Markets Limited

(b) Name of offeror/offeree in relation to whose relevant securities this form relates:

(c) Name of the party to the offer with which exempt principal trader is connected:

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0820 GMT - Broadcom shares surge Friday premarket after the maker of semiconductor and software products posted better-than-expected results for its fiscal third quarter. Broadcom stock closed 1.2% higher at $306.10 ahead of results on Thursday. Shares are up 7.3% at $328.48 Friday premarket. The company reported $15.95 billion in revenue for the quarter ended Aug. 3, up 22% on year. Analysts had forecast $15.82 billion in revenue, according to FactSet. Adjusted Ebitda came in at $10.70 billion, above a FactSet forecast of $10.47 billion. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0727 GMT - Temenos's earlier-than-expected CEO transition isn't entirely surprising, Vontobel says in a research note. The Swiss banking software company said Jean-Pierre Brulard, who took the helm in May last year, is stepping down and will be replaced on an interim basis by CFO Takis Spiliopoulos. No substantial further changes at the management team are expected after Brulard's departure despite the numerous appointments under his leadership, analyst Michael Foeth writes. "We had not considered the appointment of Jean-Pierre Brulard as a long-term solution in the first place," he says, adding that Spiliopoulos stepping in ensures continuity and that the CFO could be a potential candidate himself. Temenos needs a longer-term perspective and should announce a definitive solution sooner rather than later, he says. Shares open 13% lower. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0555 GMT - The U.S.'s cancellation of validated-end user status for Chinese units of some global chip makers could hurt the companies more than China, Jefferies analysts say in a research note. Neither Samsung nor SK Hynix makes superhigh-end memory products in their Chinese fabs, while TSMC likely produces legacy chips in China, the analysts say. These chip-making plants aren't important for China's AI efforts, they add. The analysts view the move as a U.S. response to Beijing's guidance to avoid using Nvidia's H20 chips. Jefferies thinks China might use its rare-earths leverage to get the U.S. to relax export controls on chip equipment. If Trump is willing to budge on this, it could be a big positive surprise to the market in 2026, it says.(sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0352 GMT - Yonyou Network's 2Q results suggest it is gradually recovering from the aftermath of a restructuring, Nomura analysts say. Revenue growth signals a turnaround in demand from key customers, while cost cuts pave the way for an earnings rebound in 2026. While there might be more reorganization aftershocks, the worst is likely over, Bing Duan and Joel Ying write. Nomura cuts 2025-2026 revenue forecasts by 21%-25% and earnings forecasts by 232%-61% as the software company navigates its shift to a subscription model--a change that could lead to sustained revenue growth in the next 1-3 years. Yonyou's generative AI strategy is another tailwind, particularly as Chinese policymakers promote AI use. Nomura maintains a neutral rating on the stock and raises the target to CNY16.00 from CNY12.00. Shares last at CNY14.44. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0257 GMT - Lenovo's personal AI assistant on PCs could be a game changer, UOB analysts say in a research note. Lenovo AI Now runs locally on the PC and can access local files and take actions, they say after attending an analysts' tour. Lenovo's tablets and smartphones could also give it an edge, as all products will be integrated into the AI database after the AI assistant is expanded to mobile devices in January, the analysts add. Lenovo's sales momentum for AI PCs has already surpassed expectations, with the usage rate expected to increase to 50% globally by 2026, they cite management as saying. UOB maintains a buy rating and a HK$12.70 target price on the stock, which is last at HK$10.72. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0209 GMT - Samsung Electro-Mechanics' multilayer-ceramic-capacitor division could post its ninth consecutive quarter of revenue growth in 3Q, Nomura anaysts Eon Hwang and Heesoo Min write in a note. Revenue from the company's MLCC segment is expected to rise 14% on year for the July-September quarter, with operating profit likely to increase 32% from a year earlier, the analysts reckon. They expect the MLCC market to continue to grow in the information-technology, industrial and auto sectors. The Samsung Electronics affiliate retains an estimated 40% share of the MLCC market for artificial-intelligence data servers, with its market share for auto MLCCs also rising, they note. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0104 GMT - The most pressing issue facing Australian mobile operators is the growing number of customers trading down from premium postpaid plans to prepaid services and second-tier brands, Jarden analysts say. They observe that both Telstra and TPG Telecom have acknowledged a skew in customer activity toward the lower end of the market, and think that the trend will continue into FY 2026. Telstra's success in growing prepaid and wholesale revenue-per-user at a faster rate than postpaid shows the strength of its multi-brand approach, they write in a note. Action on pricing across lower-value segments is key to further repairing industry returns, they add. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2349 GMT - Tuas's bull at Citi sees the Singapore-focused telco provider's proposed acquisition of Keppel's M1 expediting its journey from challenger to clear market leader. Analyst William Park tells clients that the addition of M1 will establish Tuas as a formidable competitor in both the premium-end and value-end of the market. He sees no shortage of revenue and cost synergies over time, including consolidation of call centers and leveraging of network scale. However, he doesn't expect any detail on these when Tuas announces its annual results this month. Citi has a buy rating and last-published A$7.10 target price on the stock, which is at A$7.58 ahead of the open. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2302 GMT - Sky Network Television's recent acquisition of Discovery NZ is both strategically smart and positive for earnings, Morgan Stanley says. As a pay TV business, Sky faces the same structural headwinds as most global peers. But there's a distinction worth highlighting to investors. "The main one being, as a small market (total population 5.3 million) it doesn't make sense for every studio to do direct-to-consumer in New Zealand," analyst Andrew McLeod says. "The scale economics don't work." So, more content suppliers are choosing to partner with Sky than go it alone in New Zealand. The Discovery NZ deal is a good example of this, MS says. The bank raises its price target on Sky by 15%, to NZ$2.29/share. Sky is down 2.6% at NZ$3.05 today. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1755 GMT - Figma has several growth drivers that have yet to play out, including the monetization of its prompt-based AI design offering, Figma Make, William Blair analysts say in a note. The feature has garnered promising early feedback after its recent launch, but the company has yet to monetize it and seems to be taking a conservative approach to guidance, the analysts say. Figma will likely be able to monetize the offering heading into 2026, driving upside to current estimates, the analysts say. "We believe this offering is critical to the future of the company and are encouraged by the good early uptake," they say, reiterating their outperform rating on the stock. Figma sinks 20% to $54.74 after sales guidance missed some analyst views. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1744 GMT - Advanced Micro Devices is struggling to grow orders from its customers, says Seaport analyst Jay Goldberg, who downgrades the stock to neutral from buy. The chipmaker is improving its MI series accelerators, but "many of their headline customers have only purchased evaluation systems that are unlikely to convert into volume orders for at least one generation of the MI systems," Goldberg says. Customers like Microsoft and Meta may also reevaluate their AI spending plans, leading to reduced orders. The company's use of discounts is also becoming more widespread and it may lose negotiating leverage with suppliers, hurting margins, Goldberg says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1730 GMT - Figma's 2Q results show the company is a leader in the growing user interface and user experience design industry, BofA Securities says in a note. The company looks well positioned to continue gaining share in the $36 billion digital design marketplace given its competitive advantages like a large base of monthly active users and paid customers, as well as its comprehensive offerings spanning whiteboarding, development, websites and social media, the analysts say. It also has a powerful set of generative AI features, which could drive further growth next year with monetization, they say. Still, the analysts lower their price target on the stock to $69 from $85 given revenue growth is decelerating, though it remains at a double-digit rate. Figma sinks 18% to $55.58. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0654 GMT - Sanofi's shares took a beating after the French pharmaceutical company reporter results from a late-stage study for eczema drug candidate amlitelimab that disappointed investors, but the reaction seems overdone, Berenberg analysts say. "The share price reaction indicates that investors were hoping for a more impactful drug profile, offering sales upgrade momentum and the ability to substantially offset the Dupixent patent expiry from 2032," the analysts say in a research note. Berenberg sees a buying opportunity in Sanofi, citing expectations of top-line growth of 7% a year to the end of the decade and a strong balance sheet. However, persistent uncertainty about the company's research-and-development programs leads the analysts to lower their target price on Sanofi to 110 euros from 118 euros. Sanofi shares fell 8.3% on Thursday to close at 78.96 euros. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

1356 GMT - Economist Omair Sharif notes a surprising result in the August private-payrolls data from ADP: The private education/health sector--without any seasonal adjustment--shed a net 50,000 jobs last month, the worst result ever for the sector. Typically, it adds about 35,000 jobs in August, Sharif notes. "It is hard to explain the collapse, " he writes. Data from the JOLTS report, released Wednesday to show data through July, indicated broad stability in this sector over the past year, so the apparent sudden decline is confusing. The official August jobs figures from the Labor Department, due Friday morning, may provide more of a clue. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1123 GMT - Sanofi's amlitelimab drug candidate might still have a role in the eczema market despite achieving weaker-than-expected results in a late-stage study, JPMorgan analysts say in a note. The French pharmaceutical company said the drug met the main goals in a study of adults and adolescents with skin condition eczema, also known as atopic dermatitis. Results for patients dosed every 12 weeks were as good as those for patients dosed every four weeks, which will offer a convenience benefit relative to existing treatment options, according to JPMorgan. It is too early to assess the commercial potential for the drug as more data is required, the analysts say. Shares fall 10%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

1044 GMT - Sanofi's amlitelimab drug candidate achieved weaker-than-anticipated results in a late-stage trial, underperforming its blockbuster medicine Dupixent for the treatment of eczema, J.P.Morgan analysts write in a note. "While the trial was successful, with a statistically significant benefit on all primary and secondary endpoints, the magnitude of the benefit on the disclosed primary endpoints of [clearance of eczema, or atopic dermatitis, from 75% of the body and skin clearance] was lower than expected and lower than Dupixent," the analysts say. The efficacy of amlitelimab in the trial seems likely to have been diluted by the fact that a substantial portion of patients were treated with advanced therapies, according to J.P.Morgan. Shares fall 10%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0715 GMT - Ashmore shares open 12% lower at 150 pence, wiping the London-listed emerging markets asset manager's year-to-date gains after it reported its full-year print. Adjusted Ebitda was lower than expectations on heavier staff costs while its top line was weaker as management fees also missed views, Jefferies says in a research note. Performance fees were in line. Emerging markets demand should be helped by a weaker dollar and concerns about how much debt developed countries have, analysts say. Signs of this have emerged over the past four to five months, and this should be positive for Ashmore, they add. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0032 GMT - IAG may have to divest its existing operations in Western Australia state if it is to secure permission for its latest acquisition from the competition regulator, Jarden analysts say. They think that the regulator's statement on its preliminary concerns was largely in line with market expectations, but cautions that such commentary has historically been followed by significant structural changes to a proposed deal. Other solutions in this case could include carving out geographical portfolios, maintaining target RACI's operational independence in Western Australia, or even shifting to a partial acquisition, the analysts write in a note. They keep a neutral rating and A$8.50 target price on IAG, which is up 1.2% at A$8.755. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2245 GMT [Dow Jones]--IAG's proposed acquisition of RAC Insurance is unlikely to proceed after the Australian competition regulator raised concerns about the deal, Morgan Stanley says. It estimates the combined share of IAG and RAC in Western Australia's motor and home insurance market could approach 50%. That is likely too high. "We think the debate starts to shift back to organic growth," says analyst Andrei Stadnik. IAG's top-line growth guidance for FY 2026 — when excluding benefits from acquisitions — is slightly softer than peers. "With less support from inorganic opportunities and fewer reinsurance levers to pull, we see IAG's earnings growth moderating alongside a slower pricing cycle," MS says. It has an equal-weight call on IAG. (david.winning@wsj.com)

1412 GMT - U.S. real estate investors purchased roughly 52,000 homes in 2Q, the lowest level for that time of year since 2020, according to Redfin. That's down 6% from a year earlier, the biggest drop since 4Q of 2023. Real estate investors are pulling back for similar reasons individual homebuyers are pulling back: high borrowing costs, elevated home prices and economic uncertainty. It's more expensive for investors to finance their purchases than it has been in the past. Even though most investors pay in cash and don't take out mortgages to buy homes, they often take on other loans to fund things like renovations, and interest rates are much higher than they were during the pandemic. Additionally, home prices remain near record highs. Asking rents have also declined from their peak in much of the country. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1406 GMT - Declining mortgage rates have pushed the median U.S. monthly housing payment down to $2,593, the lowest level since January, according to Redfin. The weekly average mortgage rate has ticked down to 6.56%, the lowest level in nearly a year. Lower monthly payments are leading to a modest uptick in home sales. Pending home sales rose 1.6% from a year earlier. But sales aren't surging, likely because it's still difficult for many Americans to afford a home. Home-sale prices rose 1.6% year over year during the four weeks ending Aug. 31, and while monthly payments have fallen from their peak, they're still up 5% from a year ago. "Mortgage rates haven't come down significantly enough to bring back a flood of buyers," Redfin says. Inventory has declined from its summer peak, one reason why sale prices are still rising. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1158 GMT - The business model of W.A.G. Payment Solutions, also know as Eurowag, looks strong, Peel Hunt analysts Gautam Pillai and Barun Singh write in a note. The U.K.-listed integrated payments and mobility platform delivered positive cash generation for the first half, with net debt falling and adjusted cash Ebitda growing 14.1% on year. The increase shows Eurowag's improving cash conversion and provides space for reinvestment and debt-reduction, they add. Shares are up 1.1% at 95.60 pence.(najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

1200 GMT - Klarna has renewed plans to list, but its pricing reflects a step down in valuation amid a more conservative environment, PitchBook senior EMEA private capital analyst Navina Rajan writes. The new price range implies a valuation of between $12.5 billion and $14 billion, down from the previously signaled target of above $15 billion, as wider market volatility has caused instability in public equity markets, Rajan adds. While the timing of the listing isn't certain, it is thought to most likely occur within the next few months, a move which would end the company's long road of IPO postponement. Klarna's potential U.S. listing follows several other tech companies that have been able to pull the IPO trigger this year, such as Chime Financial and Circle Internet, Rajan notes. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1125 GMT - W.A.G. Payment Solutions, also known as Eurowag, only confirmed its full-year guidance due to low visibility, Jefferies analyst Hannes Leitner writes in a note. With the mobility and payment company's toll business "possibly continuing its run, we could see the high end as achievable," he adds. For 2025, the U.K.-listed company expects low-teens on-year net revenue growth and an adjusted Ebitda margin in line with the previous year. Shares are up 1.1% at 95.60 pence.(najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

1037 GMT - W.A.G. Payment Solutions' prospects look positive, Berenberg analysts write in a note. The U.K.-listed integrated payments and mobility platform--also known as Eurowag--delivered a strong first-half performance, with net revenue growth 4% ahead of consensus expectations despite a challenging environment, they say. "Eurowag appears increasingly well-positioned due to the combination of its top-line momentum, improving capital discipline and liquidity management, and its new, cash-profit-focused long-term incentive plan structure," they add. Shares are up 1.1% at 95.60 pence. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0813 GMT - Orsted's downgrade to earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization is a small negative ahead of Friday's vote on a proposed 60 billion Danish kroner rights issue, Jefferies analyst Ahmed Farman writes. The company lowered its full-year Ebitda guidance to between 24 billion and 27 billion kroner, from 25 billion-28 billion kroner previously, driven by lower-than-normal offshore wind speeds during July and August. Orsted also mentioned it expects a construction delay at its Greater Changhua 2b project, which will also adversely affect Ebitda in 2025. Jefferies expects details on the rights issue in the coming days. Shares rise 0.9%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0708 GMT - Orsted's guidance downgrade is a negative ahead of the rights issue prospectus which is expected early next week, RBC analyst Alexander Wheeler writes. Following lower-than-normal offshore wind speeds, Orsted lowered its 2025 Ebitda guidance to 24 billion-27 billion Danish kroner ($3.7 billon to $4.2 billion), from 25 billion-28 billion kroner previously. Company consensus is at 26.5 billion kroner, Wheeler adds. Lower wind speeds have impacted Ebitda by 1.2 billion kroner, with a construction delay at the Greater Changhua 2b project providing a 300 million kroner hit. Additionally, commissioning of Greater Changhua 2b has been delayed from late 2025 to the third quarter of 2026 due to damage to an export cable, he adds. Shares fall 1.7%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1933 GMT - Crude futures settle lower for a second straight session as the market frets about this weekend's OPEC+ meeting and the possibility of the group raising output targets further after September. The EIA's weekly inventory report was "quite bearish to both crude and the distillates but bullish to gasoline," Ritterbusch says in a note. U.S. crude stocks rose by 2.4 million barrels against expectations of a decline. "And the fact that Cushing accounted for more than 2/3 of the increase with stocks lifting some 1.6 million barrels should accentuate negative response to today's data by forcing further weakness into the WTI curve," Ritterbusch adds. WTI settles down 0.8% at $63.48 a barrel, and Brent falls 0.9% to $66.99. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1312 GMT - Oil futures are lower with the market cautious ahead of the OPEC+ meeting at the weekend with concerns the group could agree to unwind more output cuts. "However, we are skeptical and believe that the chances of them maintaining current production levels are greater than increasing output currently," Peter Cardillo of Spartan Capital says in a note. Traders will also watch for EIA inventory data due at 12 p.m. ET after the API reported a U.S. crude stock build. Analysts in a Wall Street Journal survey expect a 2 million barrel draw in crude stocks, as well as declines in product inventories. WTI is down 1.1% at $63.27 a barrel, and Brent is off 1.2% at $66.80. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Broadcom Shares Jump Premarket After Strong Results — Market Talk
data.description: Broadcom shares surge Friday premarket after the maker of semiconductor and software products posted better-than-expected results for its fiscal third quarter. Broadcom stock closed 1.2% higher at $306.10 ahead of results on Thursday. Shares are up 7.3% at $328.48 Friday premarket. The company reported $15.95 billion in revenue for the quarter ended Aug. 3, up 22% on year. Analysts had forecast $15.82 billion in revenue, according to FactSet. Adjusted Ebitda came in at $10.70 billion, above a FactSet forecast of $10.47 billion. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0223 GMT - Iron ore futures are mixed in Asian trading as steel production resumes in northern China after Beijing's military parade. Despite the temporary production halt, Chinese steel mills' margins are lower in recent weeks, which suggests deteriorating steel demand, says CBA's Vivek Dhar in a commentary. This is likely linked to the nationwide construction activity index from China's non-manufacturing PMI contracting in August, which could point to a decline in construction, he notes. "We anticipate that China's policymakers will announce additional stimulus measures in late 3Q or early 4Q that help China's commodity‑intensive economy," he says. The most-traded iron-ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange is flat at CNY783.50 a ton. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0152 GMT - Orica's trading update appears to be a net negative, according to RBC Capital Markets. Still, it is "not sufficient to derail our Outperform view," RBC says in a note. The broker says a rise in litigation costs is unfortunate but understandable. "We accept the decision to take these as significant items during the period," the broker says. The update also provides useful qualitative commentary "for a business that is becoming increasingly challenging to model," it says. While blasting solutions volumes in Asia Pacific and North America appear softer than envisaged, margins may have improved thanks to a better mix of products and services, the broker says. RBC has a A$23.00 target on Orica. The stock is up 0.9% at A$20.94. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0139 GMT - The rally in gold prices to all-time highs prompts Morgans to lift its target on Australian miner Regis Resources. The broker raises its price target on the stock to A$5.80 from A$5.00 after baking in higher metal prices. It reiterates an accumulate rating. "Spot case forecasts have been revised to reflect the material increase in the gold price, with our scenario now set at US$3,250/oz," from US$3,000/oz previously, Morgans analyst Ross Bennett says. Bennett adds that he sees "value in producers leveraged to earnings rather than growth." Regis is up 5.2% at A$5.07. Its shares are up 99% year to date. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0125 GMT - Orica's projection of more earnings growth across all its business units is encouraging, according to Citi analyst William Park. Orica's trading update exudes optimism and is "testament to ORI's unwavering focus on commercial discipline," Park says in a note. He expects Orica's specialty mining chemicals arm to gain more momentum heading into FY 2026. Its digital solutions business also appears well placed to benefit from Orica's geographical reach and an improvement in exploration activity, Park says. Citi has a buy rating and A$20.65 target on Orica. "We remain upbeat on ORI's outlook beyond FY25," says Park. The stock is up 1.1% at A$20.985. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2237 GMT [Dow Jones]--Lotus Resources's A$65 million capital raise looks a sensible move to Ord Minnett. Lotus wants to fortify its balance sheet as it ramps up output from the Kayelekera uranium mine in Malawi. Analyst Matthew Hope had felt Lotus's finances were strong enough to manage the restart of Kayelekera. But he says Lotus is raising from a position of strength because its share price is up some 43% over the prior month. "We consider Lotus's approach prudent, as it will consume working capital in December half of 2025 with the commencement of mining and growth of product and run-of-mine inventories," Ord Minnett says. "Cash receipts are most likely due in June half of 2026." (david.winning@wsj.com)

2229 GMT [Dow Jones]--Vault Minerals's surprise decision to buy back stock is a good "back-stop" mechanism for its share price, Euroz Hartleys says. Vault didn't put a dollar value on the buyback. It said the intention is to repurchase up to 10% of its share capital over 12 months. Analyst Mike Millikan notes that Vault's share price has risen some 36% to A$0.57 since the buyback was announced. He thinks Vault could spend A$180 million on repurchasing stock. Euroz Hartleys retains a buy call on Vault, and lifts its price target by 12% to A$0.75/share. (david.winning@wsj.com)

1432 GMT - Canadian goods exports to its southern neighbors jumped 5% in July to reach the highest level in four months at roughly C$45.1 billion. That may reflect some American buyers now having no choice but to restock at a high cost after restraining their purchases and attempting to rely on inventories while awaiting a possible resolution to trade tensions, National Bank's Jocelyn Paquet suggests. However, the economist says if that's the case, then there is still a long way to go to erase the effects of the trade war. Exports to the U.S. remain down 10.3% over 12 months. And the decline in shipments isn't yet over in certain sectors directly affected by tariffs, such as aluminum, Paquet says. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1027 GMT - Palm oil futures ended higher, with the Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for November delivery gaining 7 ringgit to 4,449 ringgit a ton. Expectations of slower Malaysian output growth likely supported any gains, Kenanga Futures analysts said in a note. Phillip Nova's Darren Lim expects more volatility ahead as crude palm oil prices briefly breached a key resistance level of 4,450 ringgit a ton, but failed to hold above it. He expects a pullback towards the 4,400-ringgit-a-ton support level, noting a break above the 4,450-ringgit level could pave the way towards 4,550 ringgit a ton. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0943 GMT - Shares in London-listed gold miners are down after gold futures fell driven by concerns over the Federal Reserve's independence, RBC Capital Markets analyst Allison Enck says in a note. President Trump has been pressuring Fed Chair Jerome Powell into early rate cuts,the WSJ has reported. U.S. gold futures are around $3,530.69 a troy ounce, down 0.8% from Wednesday's record high of $3,578.50. "Our high scenario has gold at $3,544 an ounce in 3Q, $3,722 an ounce in 4Q, and $3,789 an ounce on average next year, and there could even be upside risk to these numbers if market expectations are realized and the uncertainty premium persists, which we think it will," RBC Says. Shares in Fresnillo are down 1%, followed by Antofagasta, down 0.8%, and Endeavour Mining, down 0.2%. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0148 GMT - Japan's top trade negotiator, Ryosei Akazawa, renewed his promise to take necessary measures to ease any negative effects of higher U.S. tariffs on the nation's corporate sector. President Trump on Thursday signed an order to lower tariffs on Japanese cars, which Akazawa says will likely take effect within a maximum of two weeks. While the 15% tariff will still hurt Japanese companies' profits, the clarity on the trade policy could also help stabilize the economy and support stock prices, Akazawa says. The Nikkei Stock Average is last up 0.8%. (megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

0929 GMT - Jet2's expectations for earnings before interest and taxes at the low end of its guidance range and cut to its winter seat sales guidance will cast a shadow on airline peers, RBC's Ruairi Cullinane and Jakub Glinkowski write. The U.K. holiday company has robust growth and value creation history, but cheaper enterprise value multiples, the analysts say. The market will interpret Jet2's lower guidance as negative for peers like easyJet, they add. European airline shares fell Thursday, with easyJet down 3.3% and Ryanair and Lufthansa falling 2.7% and 1.8%, respectively. Meanwhile, British Airways owner IAG shares are 1% lower. Jet2 is trading down 14% at 1,394 pence, marking its lowest price since April. (aimee.look@wsj.com)

0844 GMT - European airline shares are down after London-listed holiday company Jet2 said it expects a key profit metric to be at the lower end of guidance, and cut seat capacity due to late booking trends. Shares in budget airline easyJet are down 3.8% and leading the FTSE 100 index fallers, while British Airways owner IAG's shares are 1.2% lower. Ryanair and Lufthansa shares are down 3% and 1.8%, respectively, while Jet2 shares are trading down 14%. (aimee.look@wsj.com)

0830 GMT - BYD's recent selloff implies that market participants are wary of the Chinese automaker's weak 2Q sales volume and revenue, Macquarie analysts write in a note. The sharp drop in gross profit margin came as a surprise, as higher overseas sales and premium models were expected to mitigate weaker Ocean and Dynasty sales, they add. Beijing's campaign against excessive competition may have forced BYD to absorb more costs upfront, weighing on 2Q results, they say. Meanwhile, pricing is stabilizing and margins are recovering in 2H, they add. Planned model updates could help BYD recapture its pole position in China's EV market in the coming peak sales season, they add. Macquarie maintains its outperform rating for the stock but cuts the target price to HK$142 from HK$155. Shares last at HK$104.80. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: DNB Carnegie Access: Orexo: Promising preclinical data
data.description:
DNB Carnegie has published a new flash comment on Orexo following recent news flow.

To access the full report and subscribe to future updates, visit https://www.carnegie.se/en/commissioned-research/orexo/

data.title: Italian Stocks Decline
data.description:
The FTSE MIB fell 0.3% to around 41,800 on Friday, giving back gains from the previous session and mirroring the cautious mood across most European markets, as investors awaited the release of the US jobs report for August. The report is seen as pivotal after a series of labor indicators pointed to a softening US labor market, reinforcing bets on a possible Fed rate cut in September.

Domestically, Italian retail sales were unchanged in July, missing forecasts of a 0.4% rise and cooling from a 0.7% increase in June, signaling weaker consumer demand heading into the second half of the year.

Across the bourse, luxury and consumer names were among the early laggards, with Moncler down 2.3% and Ferrari slipping 0.9%.

Utilities also underperformed, including Hera (-0.7%) and Telecom Italia (-0.7%).

Italian banks also traded mostly in the red.

For the week, the index is on track for a second consecutive loss.

data.title: Asian currencies, stocks climb on Fed cut hopes, weak dollar
data.description:
Key points:
  • Asian FX rallies on Fed rate-cut bets, weak dollar
  • Taiwan dollar, Korean won lead Asian FX higher
  • Regional stocks climb, led by Manila, Taipei

Asian currencies strengthened against the U.S. dollar on Friday as traders positioned for a Federal Reserve rate cut this month, while equities posted broad gains following Wall Street's overnight rally to record highs.

The South Korean won strengthened 0.35% and Taiwan's dollar climbed 0.49%, leading regional currencies. Taiwan's benchmark stock index TWSE:TAIEX rose 1.3%, extending its winning streak as technology stocks rallied.

In Thailand, the parliament was set to choose a new prime minister after a court's dismissal of Paetongtarn Shinawatra from the post. The baht gained 0.25% against the greenback, while stocks in Bangkok advanced 0.99% as markets looked beyond the political uncertainty.

Trade, however, remained subdued with Malaysia and Indonesia observing public holidays.

Markets have all but priced in a quarter-point Fed rate cut at next week's policy meeting, with traders expecting 60 basis points of reductions by year-end.

The dollar index fell 0.2% to 98.11 as traders awaited U.S. jobs data later in the day. The Fed meets on September 16 and 17.

"We anticipate the Federal Reserve will ease policy over the coming months, providing room for Asian central banks to cut rates to support growth amid trade headwinds," MUFG's forex strategist Lloyd Chan said.

"Against a backdrop of a weaker U.S. dollar, we favour the yen, rupiah, and ringgit."

On Thursday, the Bank Negara Malaysia left its overnight policy rate unchanged at 2.75% as expected.

Expectations of an easier monetary environment have supported global equities. The S&P 500 rose 0.8% to finish at a record high on Thursday.

Most regional stock markets rose, with the Philippines' index climbing 0.69% and Singapore's Straits Times adding 0.38%. Japan's Nikkei gained 0.19%, while India's Nifty declined 0.43%.

The MSCI gauge of emerging market currencies (.MIEM00000CUS) was little changed this week, while a gauge of emerging Asian equities (.MIMS00000PUS) advanced more than 1%, on track for its best week since August 11.

Though Indonesian markets had some respite this week, they could face headwinds again as deadly protests over lawmakers' bonuses escalated after police killed a motorcycle taxi driver during demonstrations.

Rights groups have reported 10 deaths and over 1,000 injuries in clashes with security forces.

The rupiah gained 0.4% over four sessions on broad dollar weakness after losing more than 1% due to the protests last week. Jakarta stocks rose 0.5% this week, after losing 0.4% in the previous week.

HIGHLIGHTS:

** Indonesia to discuss fuel supply issue with private distributors

** China's central bank to inject 1 trillion yuan

data.title: Orsted Guidance Downgrade a Small Negative — Market Talk
data.description: Orsted's downgrade to earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization is a small negative ahead of Friday's vote on a proposed 60 billion Danish kroner rights issue, Jefferies analyst Ahmed Farman writes. The company lowered its full-year Ebitda guidance to between 24 billion and 27 billion kroner, from 25 billion-28 billion kroner previously, driven by lower-than-normal offshore wind speeds during July and August. Orsted also mentioned it expects a construction delay at its Greater Changhua 2b project, which will also adversely affect Ebitda in 2025. Jefferies expects details on the rights issue in the coming days. Shares rise 0.9%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)
